data.description: The following slide deck was published by Seagate Technology Holdings plc in conjunction with this event.

data.title: Amazon: Tariffs Will Strengthen The E-Commerce Empire
data.description:
Some investors might be wondering if it is too late to buy the stock of Amazon.com, Inc. (NASDAQ:AMZN) given that it has already recovered much of its losses along with the broader market. The company continues to benefit both from generative AI tailwinds to its cloud computing business, while also showing that it can increase the dominance of its e-commerce business through faster shipping speeds without sacrificing on profitability. The company is solidly profitable and maintains a net cash balance sheet.

While the near term might see some volatility to the e-commerce business due to the tariff escalations, I do not see anything stopping this growth engine over the long term. If anything, this looks like an opportunity for the company to lean into its financial and competitive strengths and take market share. I reiterate my Strong Buy rating for the stock.

AMZN Stock Price

I last covered AMZN in March where I stuck with my bullishness amidst a bursting AI bubble. The stock has returned 8% since then.

While some may be of the opinion that stocks have not fallen enough to account for the new economic risks, AMZN looks quite attractive here.

AMZN Stock Key Metrics

The e-commerce and cloud titan delivered strong Q1 results even amidst tough macro conditions. Revenues grew 9% YoY to $155.7 billion, coming in ahead of the high end of guidance of between $151 billion and $155.5 billion.

Operating income came in at $18.4 billion, also exceeding the high end of guidance of between $14 billion and $18 billion. Tariffs do not appear to have impacted either top line growth nor profitability, but it remains to be seen if there will be an impact in the remainder of the year.

The company saw its North America e-commerce segment deliver stunning 8% YoY growth with operating margins expanding 40 bps to 6.2%. The trailing twelve months operating margin stood at 6.5%, which is incredible given that the company is operating at peak shipping speeds. It is now clear that expedited shipping has not impeded the company’s ability to drive profits, allowing it to fully benefit from the associated competitive advantages.

AMZN continued to deliver solid profitable growth in its international operations, but is now lapping tougher comparables.

The cloud computing segment AWS generated 17% YoY growth, an incredible growth rate given the $120 billion annual run rate. The company also showed off the profit potential of this segment, with operating margins expanding to 39%.

AMZN ended the quarter with $95 billion of cash versus $53.4 billion of debt, representing a bulletproof net cash balance sheet.

Looking ahead, management guided for the second quarter to see up to 11% YoY revenue growth. While the tariff uncertainty may make it difficult to outperform on the top-line, I find it likely that the company outperforms on the profit guidance given that it posted an 11.8% operating margin this past quarter.

On the conference call, management noted that they “set new delivery speed records” as they delivered “more items in the same day or next day” than any other quarter in their history. Management noted exposure to the ongoing tariff negotiations, noting that many of their retailers are either buying directly from China or “buying from companies who themselves are buying from China.” Management noted that their tariff impact is “not large” as of the next quarter due to them having done “a lot of pre-buying” in the first quarter, but I expect costs to rise more significantly in the third quarter.

I would not be surprised if AMZN tries to eat some of the costs in order to continue taking market share, which would likely pressure operating margins in the near term. While the de minimis tariff was reduced to 54% down from 120%, this still reflects a significantly larger number than the prior exemption and may affect the company for several quarters until it begins lapping these comparables.

Is AMZN Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

AMZN was recently in the news due to Nike (NKE) deciding to return to the platform for the first time since 2019. While NKE is just one retailer and thus is unlikely to make a significant impact on its own, it does help contribute to the narrative that AMZN has become synonymous with retail. I expect other name brands to follow suit as they could tap into the large market funnel and fast shipping speeds.

As of recent prices, AMZN traded hands at just around 32x earnings.

That looks like a reasonable valuation given that consensus estimates call for high-single-digit top-line growth over the coming years.

At this point, it is clear that both the e-commerce and cloud businesses are high quality with the company holding strong competitive footing (curiously enough both of these had been out of favor at some point by investors over the past 5 years). I value AWS at a 12x sales multiple, placing the valuation at $1.6 trillion. I value the e-commerce business at a 2.7x sales multiple (equating to a 30x earnings multiple based on 9% long term margins), placing the valuation at around $1.6 trillion.

Summing these up, this leads to an AMZN valuation of around $290 per share. I can see upside to both of these estimates, especially as both make further progress on margin expansion.

AMZN Stock Risks

While I expect AWS to perform strongly even in a recession, the e-commerce operations may be particularly exposed to ongoing tariff impacts. It remains unclear how much the online marketplace might be disrupted by elevated import costs from China and others. While the numbers thus far have not shown impact, as a consumer myself my hunch says “a lot” of the products are likely sourced from China. It is plausible, if not likely, that the company ends up seeing material near term impact as its sellers might need to re-think their sourcing logistics, and buyers adapt to potentially higher sticker prices.

That said, I expect the company’s dominant market positioning to enable it to return to growth mode over the long term. I note that the AWS segment continues to face intense competition from other cloud titans, and it isn’t clear if generative AI ends up leading to a “winner takes most” scenario.

AMZN Stock Conclusion

The company is showing resilient top-line growth while doing right by investors in prioritizing profit margins. Both revenue and profit growth might be pressured in the near term as the e-commerce business grapples with new tariff costs, but the company’s dominant competitive positioning puts it in a position of strength from which it might even be able to accelerate its market share gains during this difficult period.

The stock finally looks cheap on conventional value metrics due to the significant gains on profitability - I see it as just a matter of time before the company ends up ramping up its share repurchase program, which might help bring more investor attention to the name. It's worth noting that the stock even was recently added by famed value investor Bill Ackman. The stock still looks cheap even based on reasonable assumptions in my sum-of-the-parts analysis, suggesting further upside.

AMZN is a core position at the Best of Breed Growth Stocks Portfolio. I reiterate my Strong Buy rating for AMZN stock.

data.title: SCHQ: Avoiding The Twin Dollar-Duration Risk
data.description:
The Schwab Long-Term U.S. Treasury ETF (NYSEARCA:SCHQ) is a rather long duration ETF. At these effective maturities is precisely where the considerable increases in interest rates are occurring. While a de-inversion of the yield curve may ordinarily be seen as a positive thing, we believe that it is coming down to several deleterious changes to the economic monetary order, and that the increase in long-term rates is a consequence of compounding worries for the US budget interacting with a monetarily dangerous political isolationism in the US that threatens utility of the dollar. The weak auction signals some of these worries, as well as the disconnect in the rally of US stocks versus the stagnating USD FX rate. Intermediate duration might be alright given the muted expectations we discussed in other coverage, but we're being careful about higher duration exposures.

SCHQ Breakdown

The effective duration of the SCHQ is 14.2 years, which is on the long side.

The yield curve has appreciably translated upwards on the longer maturities over the last 12 months, and especially in the last 6 months. The movements have been significant and changes in this part of the yield curve mean that by definition there has been a shift in how the bond market is evaluating structural factors. Inflation has been relatively muted in the latest readings, but this concerns mostly the 1-3 year outlook where there has been an overall downward translation as opposed to upwards, however for the structural factors:

  • Bond markets are likely looking at tariffs and considering the structural inflationary effect that comes from deglobalisation and trade disintegration, even if it's not total and just incremental compared to prior expectations that likely assumed globalisation with no caveats.
  • Also related to the tariffs and much more importantly is the threat to the dollar that has been caused by not only the tariffs themselves but their erratic communication. There is a general reticence towards the US at this point as trading partners have been feeling punished, particularly countries that are otherwise allies. This goes for allocators who buy dollars as well, as the lack of recovery of the dollar versus a recovery in marginal demand for US stocks to pre-Liberation Day levels reflects a reticence to come back to dollars even though major tariffs have been paused for now and many believe that quite a bit of the tariffs will be walked back. Economically, the logic would be that a tariff would make importing less appealing and therefore spare the dollar from additional sales to import products, thereby strengthening the dollar. The fact that did not happen imputes that a decline in the geopolitical power of the dollar offset the benefits of protectionism to lead to the USD's overall decline versus peer currencies like the EUR. Countries don't buy and use dollars because they believe in the US budget and the solvency of the US. It is critical that the USD maintain its utility as a reserve currency for the state of the viability of the US budget and balance sheet to be ignored.
  • The downgrade of US debt by Moody's (MCO) highlights the runaway debt (exacerbated also by new spending bills), and means that confidence and common use of the dollar is more important than ever to maintain its utility and prevent it from being evaluated like any other currency, which usually depends on the health of the sovereign balance sheet. The weak bond auction was yet another worrying indicator that dollar has taken a reputation hit (decades to build trust, a day to lose it) and that the deficit is not being properly addressed and is becoming a factor. In the worst case scenario this may accelerate transitions by some countries in adopting another effective standard currency, where the emergence of another reasonably solid currency, particularly among US allies, could be a danger to the current monetary world order.
  • Related to the above fact, the longer-term maturities are more concerned with the ability of the US to continue to refinance its debt at attractive rates, and for the deficit to not get out of control, or for at least the conditions where an out of control deficit can be ignored by USD investors (thereby assuring indefinite refinancing more driven by the demand for the utility that the USD offers as opposed to the soundness of a USD investment). We believe the markets are considering threats to the reserve currency status to some extent, even if it's just a remote risk, in the considerable re-evaluation of long-term yields.

Bottom Line

We are not trying to make a call on the USD reserve currency status. We just want to highlight that the US's finances depends on the utility of the USD as a reserve currency, which requires that the USD remains broadly in demand, in particular by foreign buyers who both buy USD to sell their currencies for the US to net import and maintain their current account deficit and financial account surplus. The discrepancy between stock and currency recovery post Liberation Day is a worry in that regard as it reflects the damage that might have already been done as a consequence of communication style, cemented by the actual fact that overall tariff rates in the US now are akin to Smoot-Hawley levels, with the US being much more import-led now than they were back then. The pause on the China tariffs has been positive news and the market was correct to receive the news as such - after all the US and Chinese current and financial accounts integrated together look very balanced and healthy. The USD is still, of course, the reserve currency standard for now and in use, but the general threat to major trade relationships that have been an assumption of decades of intense capital investment means significant impairment risk to the global business environment. For long duration bets like SCHQ, investors would be taking a risk on what could become a more common talking point. The whole market, bonds and stocks, in all developed markets, already depend quite a lot on the current monetary order which depends on the USD reserve currency status. We would not want to take an additional risk by betting directly on sensitive, long duration bonds that have a more encompassing exposure to the USD reserve currency status. There are some select markets where you can get out of the way of these risks and we'd focus on those.

data.title: Stellantis Deserves The Sell Rating As Earnings Estimates Are Tanking
data.description:
In February, I argued that it is "time to throw in the towel" on Stellantis (NYSE:STLA), downgrade shares to "Hold" on very disappointing 2024 results.

Today, I feel another downgrade for shares is warranted, as early 2025 data has trended worse than expected. Most notably, the tariff uncertainty adds a layer of complexity, with the ongoing 25% U.S. tariffs on imported vehicles and parts, implemented since April 2025, severely impacting Stellantis' North American operations. But even before tariffs, Q1 results highlight a 14% YoY Q1 revenue decline to €35.8 billion, driven by a 9% shipment drop. Looking ahead, with tariff stacking inflating costs for Stellantis' cross-border supply chain, particularly for Jeep and Ram production in Mexico and Canada, and no immediate tariff relief beyond limited April 29, 2025, adjustments, the company faces persistent margin pressure. Accordingly, estimates for FY 2026 EBIT and FCF continue to fall.

Stellantis' 8x FCF multiple might scream "cheap", but sky-high cyclical risks, tariff uncertainty, and a transformative industry shift make it a value trap, in my view. Lets have a more detailed look.

Early 2025 Data Highlights Persistent Weakness

Q1 2025 results for Stellantis underscore persistent commercial challenges in early 2025: During the period spanning from January through end of March, Stellantis generated revenues of €35.8 billion, a 14% YoY decline. The topline contraction was driven primarily by a 9% drop in shipments. Unfortunately, Stellantis doesn't publish profitability data for the first quarter; but investors should expect that profits also took a beating in Q1, as high inventory levels (1.21 million units) drag on pricing power. Investors should also note that Stellantis' North America division led the decline in shipments and revenue, down 20% and 25% YoY, respectively. North America is Stellantis key profit driver; and accordingly, weakness in this geographical segment likely causes a steep profit decline.

Moreover, I highlight that Stellantis took the decision to suspend FY25 guidance due to U.S. tariff uncertainties, suggesting that Stellantis near-term outlook leaves little room for optimism.

Sure, the market backdrop for automotive companies is challenging, and also Stellantis competitors struggle with fading commercial momentum. But Stellantis problems are still relatively worse, as indicated by market share erosion. According to data collected by Motor Intelligence and presented by Morgan Stanley, Stellantis' U.S. market share dropped to 7.2% in April 2025, down 42 basis points YoY. And market share is still trending down.

Source: Morgan Stanley, Research note on Stellantis, dated May 2nd.

To prop up sales in the face of unrelenting 25% U.S. tariffs and fierce competition from GM and Ford, the company has resorted to aggressive discounting, with incentives spiking 18% MoM to $5,776 worth of incentives per unit. As all investors understand, incentives are usually a desperate bid to compete.

Source: Morgan Stanley, Research note on Stellantis, dated May 2nd.

Estimates For FY 2026 Tumble; FCF Now <$4 Billion

In line with the above commentary, the outlook for Stellantis in FY 2026 has taken a brutal hit, with estimates crumbling under the weight of relentless headwinds that show no signs of easing. Specifically, revised forecasts now project revenue of about $185 billion, compared to about $193 billion estimated at the beginning of 2025 ($240 billion at beginning of 2024).

At the same time, EBIT estimates plummeted to $10 billion, down from $14 billion estimated in early 2025.

Stellantis' CAPEX for 2026 is projected to be aggressively higher than the company's D&A expenses. This will cause free cash flow below EBIT. In fact, according to consensus estimates, Stellantis won't likely generate more than $3.5 billion of distributable cash in 2026, with estimates having dropped aggressively over the past year (take reference from below graph).

Its debatable whether Stellantis shares are trading cheap, considering the aggressive downward trend in estimates. Currently, the company's market cap is valued at around $31 billion, suggesting a multiple on FCF of around 8x.

A Note On Upward Risk:

Despite Stellantis' grim outlook, some catalysts could spark upside. Specifically, I view a potential rollback or softening of the 25% U.S. tariffs as a major potential driver for improving investor confidence. Because lower tariffs would ease cost pressures on Jeep and Ram production, boosting profitability and FCF above the projected $3.5 billion for 2026. In addition, potentially successful new product launches in Europe and the U.S. may reverse market share losses (7.2% in April 2025) and reduce reliance on costly incentives ($5,776/unit). Additionally, a new CEO announcement could restore (some) investor confidence.

Valuation: Fair Value Around $10

Focusing on Stellantis’ valuation, I construct a residual earnings model using consensus cash EPS forecasts. With projected cash EPS for FY 2025, FY 2026, and FY 2027 at roughly $1.29, $1.24, and $1.35, respectively, and applying a 13% cost of equity with a 0% terminal growth rate, I calculate a fair value target of approximately $9.6, suggesting a 10% downside from current prices.

While the downside risk to fair value is not too big, I caution that Stellantis' EPS estimates carry higher uncertainty compared to less cyclical businesses, given the company's exposure to a volatile auto sector undergoing a transformative, decades-long shift.

Below also the updated sensitivity table, highlighting varying combinations of cost of equity (rows) and terminal growth rates (columns).

Investor Takeaway

In February, I downgraded shares to 'Hold' after a dismal 2024. Today, I feel like it's time to give Stellantis stock another rating downgrade, as the company's downward spiral accelerates with no relief in sight. In fact, early 2025 data suggests ongoing weak commercial momentum: Q1 revenue plunge 14% YoY on a 9% YoY shipment drop, to €35.8 billion. Notably, the profit-heavy North America region led the YoY decline, with shipments and revenue down 20% and 25%, respectively. Market share for Stellantis in the U.S. continued to drop through April, now at around 7.2%.

Adding to Stellantis challenges, I highlight that the 25% U.S. tariffs, are strangling Stellantis' cross-border supply chain, inflating costs for Jeep and Ram production and forcing desperate 18% MoM incentive spikes to $5,776/units. All this caused FY 2026 estimates to tank: Revenue has been slashed to $185 billion, EBIT to $10 billion, and FCF below $3.5 billion. On updated cash EPS estimates, my residual earnings model calculates a fair value at $9.6.

data.title: Lazard U.S. Convertibles Portfolio Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Overview

The US equity market receded in the first quarter, as investors trod cautiously amid growing risks and mounting uncertainty about the domestic economic outlook.

The quarter proved to be a turbulent one for global stock markets. All eyes were on the US, with initial investor enthusiasm for the Trump administration’s anticipated business-friendly policies tempered by events that had investors debating their ramifications. With surveys indicating that business and household confidence had eroded due to fears about the inflationary effects of tariffs, investors were wary that the uncertainty might lead to a pull-back in corporate and consumer spending, lowering economic growth.

The potential economic fallout from a global trade war was a major risk factor that was considered by the Federal Reserve in its monetary policy decisions. The Fed held interest rates steady at its policy meetings in January and March after lowering them sharply late last year. With price pressures still persistent, the US central bank acknowledged in March the rising pessimism among businesses and consumers and stated that it was anticipating higher inflation and slower economic growth due to the US administration’s policies. At the same time, the Fed stated that the US economy remained solid and affirmed its previous forecast of two rate cuts this year after earlier signaling that borrowing costs would remain elevated in the near term. The Fed’s comments about the health of the US economy and its interest-rate policy path, which were less hawkish than expected, sparked a brief relief rally in the US stock market. The yield on the benchmark 10-year US Treasury note ended the first quarter at 4.21%, 37 basis points (bps) lower than three months earlier.

Adding to the uncertainty in the US stock market was the surprising news in January that a Chinese start-up had developed a lower-cost artificial intelligence (AI) model that performed comparably to those created by its US Big Tech rivals. The stock prices of AI- leveraged companies came under pressure during the quarter on worries that the frenzy over the technology has led to overspending.

Investor anxiety was partially mitigated by the results from the latest earnings season, which continued to suggest that a profit recovery was well underway. According to FactSet data, 76% of the companies in the S&P 500 Index (SPX)(SP500) topped consensus estimates, exceeding the long-term average of 67%. The fourth-quarter earnings growth rate increased 18.2% from a year earlier.

Against this backdrop, the S&P 500 Index fell 4.3% in the first quarter. (Index performance is measured as a total return and in US dollar terms.)

Within the US convertible benchmark, the real estate, utilities and healthcare sectors added, while financials, industrials and technology detracted during the quarter. The total volume of new convertible bond issues in the US during the quarter amounted to USD 15 billion. The most active sectors during the quarter were technology, health care, and financials.

Portfolio Review

In the first quarter, the Lazard US Convertible Portfolio underperformed its benchmark, the ICE BofA US Convertible Index. (Excess return is measured net of fees and in US dollar terms.)

The Portfolio’s equity sensitivity was higher than the benchmark at 41.2% versus 39.6% for the benchmark. The Portfolio’s equity-sensitivity overweight was a moderate detractor from relative performance, with the returns of convertibles’ underlining equities on average negative during the quarter.

The modified duration ended the quarter similar to the benchmark at 1.65 versus 1.57 versus the benchmark. Sensitivity to interest rates had a positive absolute impact with lower interest rates in the US. The impact from credit was negative on an absolute basis with wider credit spreads. The relative impact was positive as the portfolio was underweight the poorest credits.

Selections in cruise operators, health care and live entertainment were additive on idiosyncratic factors. Positions in real estate were also additive supported by strong underlining trends and lower US interest rates. Selections in technology and industrials hurt on the back of poor quarterly results. Underweight exposure to certain financial sector names hurt, as the industry was supported by projections of less regulatory interference in the coming years. Underweight exposure to industrials also hurt on the back of increased expectations for the US economy. Finally, selections in healthcare disappointed due to poor quarterly results.

Outlook

The convertible bond market provided significant downside protection during a volatile first quarter while also outperforming global equities and bonds. For convertible bonds, several factors contributed to this positive development, including exposures to European and Asian issuers, which helped counter the general weakness in US risk assets; a lack of exposure to the so-called “Magnificent Seven” group of mega-cap US stocks, which dragged down the US equity market; and strong new issuance over the past two years that introduced convex convertible structures that proved to be protective during the volatile risk-off periods. We believe the structure of the convertible bond market and the global diversification of the asset class provides an interesting opportunity for investors looking to navigate through a highly uncertain period for financial markets.

In 2025, we believe that major central banks around the world will continue to adopt more accommodative monetary policy stances, while at the same time, the expansion on the fiscal side could be more pronounced than we initially expected. In the US, the Fed could continue to cut interest rates to support businesses and consumers but is likely to do so at a slower pace to allow for greater flexibility as it waits for further clarity about the US administration’s trade policies.

In Europe, the ECB will probably continue lowering rates in response to slowing economic growth in the eurozone and heightened geopolitical risks while greater fiscal flexibility from governments in the common currency bloc could support the industrial complex, which, in turn, could support the equities of convertible issuers from the defense, aeronautics, and infrastructure sectors. Finally, China has signaled that it will provide additional monetary and fiscal support in 2025 to boost flagging consumer spending, with the size and scope likely influenced by upcoming US trade policy. Inward looking Chinese consumer names could benefit from this support while being insulated to the uncertainty surrounding global trade. Overall, we believe that the current global monetary and fiscal cycles could provide a tailwind for underlining equities of important convertible issuers.

We believe the sector and market cap biases of US convertibles could prove favorable in the coming quarters. In particular, mid-cap names could thrive in an environment of lower taxes and looser regulations. With a higher exposure to technology, biotechnology, and consumer services, the sector distribution of the convertible bond market could benefit from the new US administration’s “America first” policies, which would bolster domestically focused companies that have less exposure to global trade at a time of mounting tariff risks. Finally, an increase in activity for mergers and acquisitions (M&A) could support convertibles due to its high share of mid-cap companies, which are usually the acquisition targets.

In our view, the structures of convertible bonds remain favorable for investors. Higher interest rates globally have enhanced the yield component of the asset class. For example, approximately 20% of the convertible bonds from the technology sector are yielding more than 5% per annum. With an important segment of these bonds maturing in 2025 and 2026, the asset class could experience a powerful pull to par, contributing to its overall performance.

Convertible bonds continue to show a significant credit spread discount when compared to similarly rated nominal bonds. In fact, with a favorable sector mix (with a bias toward growth companies) and less complex balance sheets, we expect convertible bonds to offer a more defensive exposure to credit risk and provide an added source of return.

New issuance was strong in 2024, with total issuance at $119 billion, considerably above historical pace and helping maintain a robust opportunity set. The issuance retained favorable pricing, including above-average coupons and lower overall premiums, coming from a diverse range of companies, with a strong US presence and the re-emergence of Asian issuance. Overall, we expect robust issuance to continue throughout 2025, due to high rates and high upcoming corporate maturities, which would provide an important source of both convexity and yield to the asset class.

In conclusion, we remain optimistic on the convertible bond asset class. We believe the economic backdrop will prove supportive for many of the convertible issuers while the structure of the market promotes high convexity, offering both interesting yield and important equity sensitivity, which should allow for important upside participation, but provide defense to the downside during periods of volatility.

Important Information

Please consider a fund’s investment objectives, risks, charges, and expenses carefully before investing. For more complete information about The Lazard Funds, Inc. and current performance, you may obtain a prospectus or summary prospectus by calling 800-823-6300 or going to www.lazardassetmanagement.com. Read the prospectus or summary prospectus carefully before you invest. The prospectus and summary prospectus contain investment objectives, risks, charges, expenses, and other information about the Portfolio and The Lazard Funds that may not be detailed in this document. The Lazard Funds are distributed by Lazard Asset Management Securities LLC.

Information and opinions presented have been obtained or derived from sources believed by Lazard Asset Management LLC or its affiliates (“Lazard”) to be reliable. Lazard makes no representation as to their accuracy or completeness. All opinions expressed herein are as of the published date and are subject to change.

The performance quoted represents past performance. Past performance does not guarantee future results. The current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted. An investor may obtain performance data current to the most recent month-end online at www.lazardassetmanagement.com. The investment return and principal value of the Portfolio will fluctuate; an investor’s shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost.

Different share classes may have different returns and different investment minimums.

Please click here for standardized returns:

https://www.lazardassetmanagement.com/us/en_us/funds/mutual-funds/lazard-us-convertibles-portfolio/f5021/s320/

Allocations and security selection are subject to change.

Mention of these securities should not be considered a recommendation or solicitation to purchase or sell the securities. It should not be assumed that any investment in these securities was, or will prove to be, profitable, or that the investment decisions we make in the future will be profitable or equal to the investment performance of securities referenced herein. There is no assurance that any securities referenced herein are currently held in the portfolio or that securities sold have not been repurchased. The securities mentioned may not represent the entire portfolio.

The ICE BofA All US Convertibles Index is a market-cap weighted index that consists of convertible bonds traded in the US dollar denominated investment grade and non-investment grade convertible securities of US companies sold into the US market and publicly traded in the United States. The Index constituents are market value weighted based on the convertible securities prices and outstanding shares, and the underlying index is rebalanced monthly. The index is unmanaged and has no fees. One cannot invest directly in an index.

An investment in bonds carries risk. If interest rates rise, bond prices usually decline. The longer a bond’s maturity, the greater the impact a change in interest rates can have on its price. If you do not hold a bond until maturity, you may experience a gain or loss when you sell. Bonds also carry the risk of default, which is the risk that the issuer is unable to make further income and principal payments. Other risks, including inflation risk, call risk, and pre-payment risk, also apply. Securities in certain non- domestic countries may be less liquid, more volatile, and less subject to governmental supervision than in one’s home market. The values of these securities may be affected by changes in currency rates, application of a country’s specific tax laws, changes in government administration, and economic and monetary policy. Derivatives transactions, including those entered into for hedging purposes, may reduce returns or increase volatility, perhaps substantially. Forward currency contracts, and other derivatives investments are subject to the risk of default by the counterparty, can be illiquid and are subject to many of the risks of, and can be highly sensitive to changes in the value of, the related currency or other reference asset. As such, a small investment could have a potentially large impact on performance. Use of derivatives transactions, even if entered into for hedging purposes, may cause losses greater than if an account had not engaged in such transactions.

Equity securities will fluctuate in price; the value of your investment will thus fluctuate, and this may result in a loss. Securities in certain non-domestic countries may be less liquid, more volatile, and less subject to governmental supervision than in one’s

home market. The values of these securities may be affected by changes in currency rates, application of a country’s specific tax laws, changes in government administration, and economic and monetary policy. Small- and mid-capitalization stocks may be subject to higher degrees of risk, their earnings may be less predictable, their prices more volatile, and their liquidity less than that of large-capitalization or more established companies’ securities. Emerging markets securities carry special risks, such as less developed or less efficient trading markets, a lack of company information, and differing auditing and legal standards. The securities markets of emerging markets countries can be extremely volatile; performance can also be influenced by political, social, and economic factors affecting companies in these countries.

Certain information contained herein constitutes “forward-looking statements” which can be identified by the use of forward-looking terminology such as “may,” “will,” “should,” “expect,” “anticipate,” “target,” “intent,” “continue,” or “believe,” or the negatives thereof or other variations thereon or comparable terminology. Due to various risks and uncertainties, actual events may differ materially from those reflected or contemplated in such forward-looking statements.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Ginkgo Bioworks: Cost-Cutting Efforts Aren't Enough
data.description:
While cost-cutting efforts are helping to trim Ginkgo Bioworks' (NYSE:DNA) losses, the demand environment remains challenging and Ginkgo's business continues to tread water. In particular, Biosecurity revenue remains under pressure and revenue from newer Cell Engineering offerings is still low.

I previously suggested that 2025 was a make-or-break year for Ginkgo Bioworks. While the company has had success shedding costs, its losses remain large and it has a dwindling cash balance. Ginkgo's stock is down around 20% since then and I expect it to remain under pressure, as investors digest the possibility that cash burn will remain elevated for the foreseeable future.

Market Conditions

In addition to general biopharma weakness that has persisted over the past few years, Ginkgo is facing increased uncertainty from changes in government policy in relation to research spending and biosecurity. Ginkgo has 28 government projects with in excess of $180 million contracted backlog, potentially putting its business at substantial risk.

Little has been said about the potential impact of tariffs. While Ginkgo could face higher prices for some of its inputs, if customers are restricted from accessing services from the likes of WuXi, it would be a significant tailwind.

Ginkgo Bioworks Business Updates

While Ginkgo continues to land programs that are similar in nature to its past programs, these are few and far between at the moment. This includes a two-year contract with ARPA-H that is worth $29 million. Ginkgo is developing a cell-free system using wheat germ that could enable low-cost biopharma production in the US. Most drugs and APIs are currently manufactured internationally, creating supply chain vulnerabilities.

Ginkgo also recently achieved a project milestone in its collaboration with Phytolon, which grants it additional equity in Phytolon. This project is focused on increasing the efficiency of natural food color production.

Cell Engineering activity continues to shift towards projects that are smaller in scope, driven by Ginkgo's Automation, AI and Datapoints products. For example, early customers, including Genentech and Recursion, are using automated data generation in support of drug discovery.

Ginkgo's RACs (Reconfigurable Automation Carts) are also generating revenue. It is worth noting that these came from the acquisition of Zymergen and represent an enormous shift from Ginkgo's previous positioning as the AWS of biology. Octant has been using RACs for drug discovery for the past two years and has seen a 7x increase in throughput and an 88% reduction in hands-on time. Ginkgo is also starting to see adoption in diagnostics, with Ginkgo now designing RACs for Aura Genetics, a diagnostic company targeting the at-home testing and wellness market.

Twist Bioscience (TWST) and Ginkgo recently updated their partnership agreement. The new three-year, $15 million agreement provides Ginkgo with preferential pricing and no required minimum purchase volume for Twist's DNA products in return for annual prepayments. Twist will also receive licenses and rights to certain long DNA technologies and reagents. This is likely related to IP that Ginkgo obtained through the acquisition of Gen9 in 2017. Ginkgo can continue to utilize the IP and is presumably receiving more favorable pricing in return for giving Twist the licenses and rights.

Financial Analysis

Ginkgo's Cell Engineering revenue in Q1 totaled 438 million. Approximately 47.5 million of this was non-cash deferred revenue recognized from BiomEdit though. Ginkgo's agreement BiomEdit was terminated, and the revenue was an accounting necessity rather than something of actual value. Excluding this, Cell Engineering revenue only increased 10% YoY. Biosecurity revenue totaled $10 million, roughly flat YoY.

Ginkgo had 123 revenue generating programs in Q1, up 32% YoY, with growth dominated by biopharma customers. These programs are smaller in nature though, and hence Ginkgo's revenue growth remains weak. Datapoints deals are smaller and there are only a small number of Automation deals at the moment. Tools only contributed low single-digit million dollar revenue in the first quarter, against expectations of low double-digit million dollar revenue for the full year.

Ginkgo raised its full-year revenue guidance to $167-$187 million, which would be fairly flat YoY. The increase in guidance is only due to the BiomEdit non-cash revenue though. Cell Engineering is expected to be $117-$137 million, while Biosecurity revenue is expected to be $50 million.

Ginkgo's Biosecurity gross margin in the first quarter was 28%, excluding stock-based compensation. While this is somewhat a function of depressed revenue, the performance of Ginkgo's Biosecurity business in a post-COVID environment suggests that this is a low-value business providing commoditized services. Ginkgo is also now moving Cell Engineering costs into the COGS line item, with cost of other revenue totaling approximately $4 million in Q1.

Operating expenses declined significantly due to restructuring efforts. Ginkgo has already reduced its costs by $205 million on an annual run rate basis, against a $250 million total cost reduction target. Ginkgo is still incurring $12 million of costs in Q1 related to excess lease space, a figure that increased sequentially. It could be difficult for Ginkgo to reduce this cost given the current state of biopharma markets and the oversupply of commercial real estate in Boston.

Ginkgo's adjusted EBITDA loss was $47 million in Q1 and its cash burn totaled $58 million, although cash burn has benefited from favorable movements in working capital in recent quarters. Ginkgo is still targeting adjusted EBITDA breakeven by the end of 2026, but this seems unlikely given current losses and Ginkgo's lack of growth. This isn't overly important in the near-term due to Ginkgo's $517 million liquidity position. Investors will increasingly focus on Ginkgo's cash burn and cash balance over the next few years though, and if continued progress isn't made, the company's share price is likely to remain under pressure.

Conclusion

After bottoming in mid to late 2024, Ginkgo's share price appeared to be recovering, more than tripling from the lows. This was driven more by sentiment than a fundamental shift in Ginkgo's business though. As a result, Ginkgo's share price has come under pressure again in 2025. Ginkgo's share price has been highly correlated with Twist Bioscience's stock in recent months, suggesting that macro factors are dominating movements at the moment.

Outside of a lack of investor appetite for speculative biotech stocks, Ginkgo is yet to establish that it has a viable business. The move away from downstream value participation and the introduction of services that are smaller in scope hasn't really paid off yet. While losses are narrowing, Ginkgo's growth is weak. The new business model also shifts value creation upfront, meaning large and persistent losses are no longer tolerable. It has also taken most of the upside off the table.

data.title: GABF: Best Of Breed Financial Equities ETF
data.description:
GABF strategy

Gabelli Financial Services Opportunities ETF (NYSEARCA:GABF) is an actively managed ETF launched on 5/10/2022 with an objective of capital appreciation. GABF has a portfolio of 40 stocks and a dividend yield of 4.28%. Based on the prospectus, the total expense ratio is only 0.10%, corresponding to acquired fund expenses. Indeed, the 0.90% management fees are totally waived by the Adviser (possibly temporarily).

As described by Gabelli, to be eligible, a company must devote at least 50% of its assets to, or derive at least 50% of its revenues from financial services. The fund applies a proprietary methodology known as Private Market Value with a Catalyst™. Eligible companies are selected based on factors such as price, earnings expectations, earnings and price histories, balance sheet characteristics, and perceived management skills, also taking into account economic and political outlooks. Then, only companies with a catalyst are added in the portfolio.

The portfolio turnover was 31% in the most recent fiscal year. This article will use as a benchmark the largest capital-weighted ETF in the sector: Financial Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLF).

Portfolio

GABF is almost exclusively invested in U.S. companies (about 95% of asset value), with about 49% in large and mega caps, 30% in mid-caps and 21% in small and micro caps. Compared to the sector benchmark, the portfolio is overweight in the capital markets industry (44.1%).

The portfolio is very concentrated: the top 10 issuers, listed in the next table, represent 49.8% of asset value, and each of them weighs between 4% and 7%.

Takeaway

Gabelli Financial Services Opportunities ETF has a portfolio of 40 financial stocks, selected based on fundamentals and catalysts. GABF is quite concentrated in the capital markets industry and in its top holdings. The track record of GABF is too short to assess its strategy, but it is promising. Since its inception 3 years ago, GABF has outperformed the sector benchmark and its competitors, making it more attractive than most, if not all other financial equities ETFs. The fund's low AUM and trading volumes are notable risks, though.

data.title: Workday, Inc. 2026 Q1 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Workday, Inc. in conjunction with their 2026 Q1 earnings call.

data.title: Vital Energy: Hedges Protect Its 2025 Free Cash Flow
data.description:
Vital Energy, Inc. (NYSE:VTLE) is now projected to generate around $267 million in 2025 free cash flow at current strip. This is only modestly lower than what I had projected for Vital before oil prices (CL1:COM) dropped in April.

Vital is 94% hedged on oil in 2H 2025, so changes in oil prices don't really have any net impact on its free cash flow then.

Vital's hedging position is much smaller in 2026 though, so it is relying on service cost decreases to reduce its corporate breakeven level. If service costs decrease by 10%, it may be able to generate close to $150 million in 2026 free cash flow at current strip.

An indefinite $60 WTI oil environment would likely leave Vital's equity with quite limited value. I am modeling a scenario where oil rebounds to $70 by 2027 though. That scenario should allow Vital to manage its debt and I'd estimate its value at around $34 per share in that scenario (if it isn't forced to dilute to reduce its net debt while oil prices are lower).

Q1 2025 Results

Vital reported relatively strong Q1 2025 production results. During the quarter it averaged 140,159 BOEPD in total production, including 64,893 barrels per day of oil production. This was 2% above the midpoint of its total production guidance and 1% above the oil production guidance midpoint for the quarter.

Vital also expected $115 million to $120 million in lease operating expenses in Q1 2025 and ended up well below that at $103 million. Vital attributed that to lower workover activity during the quarter as well as expenses with its Point Energy assets coming in lower than previously estimated. There was also a $6 million adjustment related to previous periods that reduced its reported lease operating expenses in Q1, so Vital's lease operating expenses would have been close to $110 million without that adjustment.

Vital is taking a relatively conservative approach in projecting its future lease operating expenses and is maintaining its expectations at around $115 million per quarter for the rest of 2025. This allows for the impact of increased water volumes during the rest of the year.

Reagan County Divestiture

Vital made a minor divestiture of non-core Reagan County assets for $20.5 million in cash. This involved 9,100 net acres with current production of 1,300 BOEPD (12% oil).

The divested acreage didn't include any of Vital's inventory locations and appears to part of the gas-weighted legacy position that it stopped developing years ago after starting its acquisitions of oil-weighted inventory.

The divested assets can probably generate around $4 million to $5 million in EBITDA per year (with a low base decline rate) at $60 to $70 WTI oil and assuming a realized price of $12 per BOE for the non-oil production.

Vital also noted that its asset retirement obligation was reduced by $8.4 million from the sale.

Notes On Hedges

Vital is heavily hedged on oil and gas for the rest of 2025. It has swaps covering around 83% of its Q2 2025 oil production and approximately 94% of its 2H 2025 oil production. After factoring in the effect of production taxes, oil price changes during the second half of 2025 would have effective zero direct effect on Vital's free cash flow.

Vital is also heavily hedged on natural gas and propane and ethane for the rest of 2025, so it is only the other NGL components (butane, isobutane and pentanes/natural gasoline) that it has significant price exposure to during 2025. Those other NGL components only account for around 6% to 7% of Vital's total production.

Vital currently has a lot more exposure to commodity prices changes in 2026. It is around 17% hedged on 2026 oil production if production growth is flat compared to 2025, although it seems likely that Vital's oil production will decline at least a little in 2026 if oil remains around $60.

It is also unhedged on NGLs in 2026, although it does have hedges covering approximately 56% of its 2026 natural gas production (based on 2025 production levels).

Updated 2025 Outlook

Vital has maintained its guidance for 2025 so far. Its production outperformance should offset the impact of its Reagan County divestiture, so I am continuing to model its 2025 production at 137,500 BOEPD, including 64,500 barrels per day in oil production.

Vital appears more likely to reduce development starting late in 2025 to early 2026 if oil prices don't improve noticeably by then.

The current strip for 2025 is around $63 to $64 WTI oil (including roughly $61 WTI oil during the last three quarters of 2025).

I project Vital's 2025 revenues at $2.071 billion at current strip prices, inclusive of hedges.

Barrels/Mcf $ Per Barrel/Mcf $ Million
Oil 23,542,500 $64.25 $1,513
NGLs 13,505,000 $15.75 $213
Natural Gas 78,840,000 $1.50 $118
Hedge Value $227
Total Revenue $2,071

This results in a projection of $267 million in 2025 free cash flow for Vital.

$ Million
Lease Operating Expense $460
Production and Ad Valorem Taxes $120
Marketing, Gathering and Transportation $74
Cash G&A $90
Net Interest $185
Capital Expenditures $875
Total Expenses $1,804

Debt Situation

Vital expects to put approximately $300 million towards debt reduction in 2025, including the proceeds from its Reagan County divestiture. This would reduce Vital's net debt to around $2.14 billion by the end of 2025, consisting of $1.6 billion in notes and $540 million in credit facility debt (net of cash).

Vital's credit facility borrowing base was reduced from $1.5 billion to $1.4 billion, but Vital still has plenty of room under that facility.

Vital mentioned that it believed its corporate breakeven level could be reduced to $53 oil in 2026 if service costs come down 10% in a continued $60 oil environment. This could allow Vital to generate near $150 million in free cash flow in 2026 at $60 oil.

I believe Vital would be better off by reducing its development pace in 2026 if oil remains around $60 though, so that it can preserve more of its inventory and further increase its free cash flow.

Vital's leverage could end up around 1.8x at the end of 2026 at $60 WTI oil. This is higher than ideal, but at least its largest note maturity isn't until 2032.

Notes On Valuation

I had previously estimated Vital's value at $47 per share at long-term (after 2025) $75 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas.

I have reduced its estimated value to $34 per share now, based on long-term (after 2026) $70 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas.

The reduction in estimated value is due to the $5 decrease in the long-term oil price as well as the assumption that lower oil prices continue through 2026.

Vital's estimated share value is more than double its current $15 share price. Debt makes up the majority of Vital's enterprise value though. After factoring in its net debt, an estimated value of $34 is around 25% to 30% higher in terms of enterprise value than a $15 share price.

Conclusion

Vital Energy's operational performance has been solid and it is very heavily hedged for 2025. This allows it to still generate around $267 million in projected free cash flow in 2025 despite oil strip prices dropping to around $60 for the majority of the year.

Vital expects service costs to come down by around 10% in a continued $60 WTI oil environment, which would then allow it to generate close to $150 million in 2026 free cash flow at $60 WTI oil despite limited hedges.

Vital's leverage is significant at $60 WTI oil, though, and a situation with indefinite $60 WTI oil would leave quite limited value for its equity. Vital does have a bit of time for oil prices to rebound though, with its next note maturity in 2029 and its largest note maturity coming in 2032.

I am modeling a scenario where oil prices remain at around $60 until the end of 2026 and then rebound to $70 after that time. I believe that Vital can be worth around $34 per share in that scenario.

data.title: Motorcar Parts Of America: New Margins, Old Problems (Ratings Upgrade)
data.description:
Thesis

Motorcar Parts of America (NASDAQ: NASDAQ:MPAA) is one of those legacy suppliers in the auto aftermarket that’s been around long enough to seem dependable by default. It makes the essential components like starters, alternators, brake systems, diagnostics, sold to both retail chains and pro installers across North America and abroad. The company has a global manufacturing footprint and a foothold in the kind of vehicle maintenance that can’t really be deferred.

And yet, for all that baked-in relevance, MPAA has spent years stuck in a loop of operational drag and underwhelming returns. Stable on the surface, inefficient underneath.

Last year, I gave a “Sell” rating on MPAA, based on what I saw as a long history of financial underperformance, declining earnings, and a stock price that had cratered since 2016 with no dividends to offset the pain. Despite some encouraging signs like expanding product lines and international growth, I felt the risks far outweighed the potential upside. That call, as it turns out, aged badly. One of my worst.

Since then, the stock’s roared back, a move that made it clear I misread both the company’s staying power and the market’s appetite for a turnaround. It was a clean miss and a reminder of how easy it is to get the timing wrong when a perennial underperformer starts to get its act together. What’s changed is substance: MPAA has restructured its operations, expanded into higher-margin categories, and deployed capital with newfound discipline, suggesting this is no longer a company struggling to stay afloat, but one building a foundation for sustained value creation.

Missed the Mark

Back in October 2024, when my “Sell” call dropped, I thought the rationale was airtight: this was a company with a massive stock collapse, no dividend to distract from the bruises, and a record of earnings volatility that made forecasting an exercise in futility. Despite its global footprint and its portfolio of seemingly indispensable automotive aftermarket parts like starters, alternators, calipers, pads, MPAA looked like it was jogging in place, getting nowhere while its cost base ballooned and consumers pulled back amid macroeconomic malaise.

Yet, since that nadir, the stock has come back viciously, much to the chagrin of my ego, over 90%. That number demands interrogation because the business fundamentals have changed. There’s been a measurable realignment under the hood, and that starts with earnings.

In Q3 of fiscal 2025, MPAA reported $186.2 million in revenue, a year-over-year jump of 8.3%. Not only did this beat consensus by $6.68 million, but it did so in a quarter that typically lags due to seasonal automotive demand patterns.

Gross profit climbed to $44.9 million, a year-over-year gain of 49.4%. Just as crucially, the company’s gross margin jumped from 17.5% to 24.1%, this despite $3.4 million in non-cash charges tied to inventory revaluation and amortization.

Leaner Operations

How did they pull that off? The headline move was operational relocation: shifting production and distribution from Torrance, California to Mexico. That decision slashed overhead, introduced more automation, and allowed for tighter cost control without throwing new capital at the problem. As a result, MPAA generated $34.4 million in cash flow from operations in the quarter. They didn’t hoard it either. They reduced net bank debt by 26%, cutting it from $114.3 million to $84 million. On top of that, they bought back 268,130 shares for $2.1 million at an average price of $7.82.

Then there’s the portfolio expansion. Early 2025 saw MPAA add 120 new part numbers, expanding its coverage to an additional 30 million vehicles in operation. The company’s offerings like alternators, starters, brake calipers, pads, and rotors are being pushed under the Quality-Built and Pure Energy banners, both of which have real cachet in the repair shop ecosystem. Take the brake segment. This used to be a supplementary play, but now it’s MPAA’s second-largest category. The brake caliper facility, which wasn’t even operating at scale initially, has outperformed projections. Its effective capacity has grown by more efficient processes. The result: rising output, improving margins, and zero need for major new capital.

The rotating electrical segment like alternators and starters remains the bedrock. These are parts you replace when they break, not when you feel like it. As the average age of a U.S. vehicle reaches 12.8 years, these replacements become unavoidable. They don’t depend on economic confidence or discretionary spending. If your starter dies, your car is a paperweight.

Diagnostic Future

Yet perhaps the most forward-looking strategy is MPAA’s foray into diagnostics. The JBT-1 benchtop tester is the spearhead here, a diagnostic device aimed at repair professionals. Management’s ambition is to turn this segment into a $100 million revenue generator. But what makes it strategically potent is the recurring revenue potential from software updates, maintenance, and service contracts. This shift toward a SaaS-like margin profile could offer a hedge against the very earnings volatility that torpedoed MPAA’s credibility in past years.

Meanwhile, the Dixie brand is pushing MPAA into the heavy-duty aftermarket: tractors, big rigs, transit fleets, construction equipment. These are high-barrier sectors: low volume, high value, and big headaches for competitors. By establishing a foothold here, MPAA is insulating itself from the commodified chaos of traditional auto parts retail. Moreover, its expanded Mexico footprint adds another layer of risk mitigation. Revenue generated in pesos is now being reinvested locally, reducing reliance on currency hedges and forward contracts. This acts as a buffer against global FX volatility, especially as dollar strength and trade policy shifts create new fault lines.

Finally, there’s the question of geopolitical and supply chain resilience. MPAA’s manufacturing triad of the U.S., Mexico, and Malaysia gives it the kind of geographic diversification that many rivals lack. It’s strategic insurance.

MPAA’s Valuation

At a blended P/E ratio of 16.62x, MPAA trades like a terminal value fossil. But its fair value estimates stretch from $67.13 all the way to $157. Assuming consensus forecasts hold even partially, the implied annual return tops 169%.

That’s lottery ticket territory.

By March 2025, earnings per share are expected to grow by 29.3%, followed by a massive 200% surge by March 2026. If these gains materialize, they’ll compress the forward P/E from 21.26 in 2025 to just 7.09 by 2026, a triple-digit shift without multiple contraction.

And yet, the engine sputters when the hood is lifted. For all the bullish projections, the company posted a trailing twelve-month net loss of $17.4 million.

At the same time, it reported $27.1 million in operating cash flow. The catch? $17.4 million of that came from deferred revenue. That's money counted as cash but not yet earned. This allows MPAA to maintain a semblance of cash health while continuing to bleed at the net level.

The company's levered free cash flow for the TTM, which was, for all intents and purposes, flat. That might not sound damning, until you realize this follows a steep FCF decline in 2023, and a longer trendline of negative free cash flow across most of the past decade. In TTM, capital expenditures were kept to a frugal $2.3 million, yet even this restraint didn’t translate to positive FCF. The reason’s not hard to spot: MPAA’s working capital like receivables, inventory, and payables are shifting unpredictably and making it hard to pin down anything resembling a reliable cash cycle. What looks like discipline starts to feel more like drift.

Quarterly results, in theory, should provide some clarity. In Q3 of fiscal 2025, MPAA did post a profit: $2.3 million in net income, or $0.11 per diluted share. However, this came up short of expectations.

EPS clocked in at $0.35, missing consensus by $0.08. Once again, the gap is largely explained by $5 million in non-cash charges. That persistent interference continues to cloud the picture of whether the company is actually making money or just creating the illusion of it through accruals and adjustments.

FX and Cost Pressures

Let's go back to FX exposure, which might actually be the most glaring red flag. In the same quarter, MPAA recorded a $2.5 million non-cash foreign exchange loss, a reversal from the $3.1 million gain it posted in the same period a year earlier. This was driven primarily by the remeasurement of U.S. dollar-denominated lease liabilities in its Mexican operations, specifically $1.9 million attributed to lease liability adjustments and another $585,000 from losses on peso forward contracts. These currency shocks shaved $3.4 million off gross profit and slashed $5 million from net income.

Operating expenses didn’t offer much refuge either. They ballooned from $20.5 million in the prior year to $27.3 million. Even after stripping out the non-cash and FX exposure, they still stood at $23.6 million, virtually flat against last year’s adjusted $24.2 million. Revenues may be up, but they are being shadowed closely by cost growth, suggesting that the company is expanding with the parking brake on.

Cumulatively, over the first nine months of fiscal 2025, MPAA has racked up a net loss of $18.7 million, or $0.95 per share. Granted, that’s an improvement over the same period last year, when the company posted a huge $50.6 million loss. But again, nearly half of the current year’s loss, $22.4 million, is tied up in non-cash charges. Add another $3.3 million in one-time expenses, and it becomes evident that profitability is being defined as much by what’s excluded as by what’s earned.

Still, MPAA looks good on paper when benchmarked against its sector. EBITDA growth of 26.7% absolutely dwarfs the Consumer Discretionary sector median of just 1.76%. That would suggest some authentic operational strength, if not for the glaring contradiction that forward EBIT is projected to decline by 11.32%. In other words, we’re simultaneously being told that the company is improving and deteriorating, depending on which end of the income statement you look at.

Final Takeaway

All things considered, I’m upgrading MPAA to a Hold. The turnaround seems real. We have operational improvements, margin expansion, and promising new diagnostics and brake segments all point to strategic progress. But the core financials are still a mess with erratic cash flows, persistent non-cash charges, and FX volatility muddying the earnings picture. So until those stabilize and material earnings growth becomes tangible, I’m not buying, but I’m not selling either.
